a.name: Typeform
a.tagline: Conversational forms with one question per screen

b.name: Google Forms
b.tagline: Free, unlimited surveys baked into Google Workspace

title: Typeform vs Google Forms (2026): Honest Side-by-Side
description: Typeform feels premium and converts well; Google Forms is free forever and integrates with Sheets. Here's which one actually wins your use case.
intro: Typeform and Google Forms sit at opposite ends of the form-builder spectrum. Typeform charges for design polish and completion rates; Google Forms gives you unlimited responses for $0 as long as you have a Gmail account. We compared free tiers, pricing, logic, design, integrations, analytics and team features so you can pick without guessing.

highlights.label: Free tier responses
highlights.a: 10 responses/month on the free plan
highlights.b: Unlimited responses, unlimited forms

highlights.label: Form design
highlights.a: One-question-per-screen, custom themes, fonts, brand colors
highlights.b: Plain Material design, limited theming

highlights.label: Completion rates
highlights.a: Reports ~57% average completion thanks to conversational flow
highlights.b: Lower completion on long forms (no progressive disclosure)

highlights.label: Conditional logic
highlights.a: Branching, calculations, hidden fields on paid plans
highlights.b: Basic section branching only

highlights.label: Price
highlights.a: Basic from $25/mo, Plus $50/mo, Business $83/mo (billed yearly)
highlights.b: Free forever; Workspace from $6/user/mo if you want admin controls

highlights.label: Data export
highlights.a: CSV, Google Sheets, webhook on paid tiers
highlights.b: Native Sheets sync, instant CSV, pivot tables out of the box

highlights.label: Integrations
highlights.a: 300+ via native + Zapier (Slack, HubSpot, Notion, Mailchimp)
highlights.b: Workspace-native + Zapier, plus Apps Script for custom logic

highlights.label: Mobile experience
highlights.a: Full-screen, swipe-friendly, optimized for phones
highlights.b: Functional but cramped on small screens

verdict: If you're a marketer, freelancer, or anyone collecting leads where design matters, Typeform earns its price tag through completion rates. If you're a teacher, a small team, or anyone who just needs answers in a spreadsheet, Google Forms is impossible to beat at $0. If neither fits, moomz is the lightweight free option for one-off social polls.
pickA: Pick Typeform if you need premium-feeling forms for lead capture, customer feedback, or anything client-facing where bounce rate matters.
pickB: Pick Google Forms if you want unlimited responses for free, native Sheets sync, and don't care that the design is utilitarian.

faq.q: Is Typeform really worth $25/month over free Google Forms?
faq.a: Only if you're using forms for revenue-adjacent work. Typeform's higher completion rate often pays for itself on lead-gen forms; for internal surveys, Google Forms is fine.

faq.q: Can Google Forms do conditional logic like Typeform?
faq.a: Sort of. You can send respondents to different sections based on multiple-choice answers, but you can't do calculations, scoring branches, or hide fields dynamically the way Typeform does.

faq.q: Which one handles file uploads better?
faq.a: Both support file uploads, but Google Forms requires respondents to sign in with a Google account. Typeform allows anonymous uploads on paid plans.

faq.q: Are responses limited on Google Forms?
faq.a: No. You get unlimited forms and unlimited responses for free. The only cap is Google Drive storage for attached files.

faq.q: Can I embed Typeform on my website?
faq.a: Yes, via iframe, popover, slider or full-page embed. Google Forms also embeds via iframe but looks less integrated with custom sites.

faq.q: Which is better for academic research?
faq.a: Google Forms, because it's free at any scale, exports cleanly to Sheets, and most universities already provide Workspace accounts.

a.name: Strawpoll
a.tagline: Quick public polls with multi-vote, ranked choice, no sign-up

b.name: Doodle
b.tagline: Group scheduling polls for meetings and events

title: Strawpoll vs Doodle: Polls vs Scheduling (2026)
description: Strawpoll and Doodle both run polls, but they solve different problems. One picks opinions; the other picks meeting times. Here's the clear breakdown.
intro: Strawpoll and Doodle get lumped together because both are 'polls,' but they target very different jobs. Strawpoll is built for opinion polls (best pizza, who should win, ranked-choice voting on a band lineup). Doodle is built for one thing: finding a time when 8 people are all free. We compared use cases, pricing, anti-abuse features and integrations.

highlights.label: Opinion polls
highlights.a: Single-vote, multi-vote, ranked choice, image polls
highlights.b: Not really designed for opinions; date/time grid only

highlights.label: Scheduling
highlights.a: No native calendar integration
highlights.b: Google/Outlook/iCloud calendar sync, time-zone aware

highlights.label: Free tier
highlights.a: Unlimited polls, ads on free plan
highlights.b: 1 active poll at a time on free, ads, limited features

highlights.label: Paid pricing
highlights.a: Pro from $19/mo, Business $69/mo (billed yearly)
highlights.b: Pro from $6.95/user/mo, Team $8.95/user/mo (billed yearly)

highlights.label: Anti-abuse
highlights.a: IP duplicate detection, CAPTCHA, login-required option, deadman switch
highlights.b: Email-based responses, less aggressive duplicate prevention

highlights.label: Reminders
highlights.a: Optional email when poll closes
highlights.b: Automatic reminders to non-responders, deadline nudges

highlights.label: Embed & share
highlights.a: Embed on Reddit, Discord, websites; short share links
highlights.b: Email invite-first; embedding less common

highlights.label: Mobile UX
highlights.a: Mobile-friendly web, no native app
highlights.b: Native iOS/Android apps + responsive web

verdict: These tools don't really compete. If you're asking 'what should we name the band' you want Strawpoll. If you're asking 'when can the five of us meet' you want Doodle. Picking the wrong one creates friction either way. If neither fits, moomz is the lightweight free option for one-off social polls.
pickA: Pick Strawpoll for opinion polls, fan votes, Discord/Reddit polls, or anything where you need ranked-choice or multi-vote logic.
pickB: Pick Doodle for scheduling meetings, organizing group dinners, or any decision that revolves around a calendar.

faq.q: Can Strawpoll do scheduling polls?
faq.a: Not natively. You can manually list dates as poll options, but you won't get calendar sync, time-zone conversion or reminder emails.

faq.q: Can Doodle handle opinion polls?
faq.a: Technically yes through its 'Yes/No/Maybe' format, but it lacks ranked choice, image options, and the share-anywhere flow Strawpoll provides.

faq.q: Does Strawpoll require sign-up?
faq.a: No. Creators can create polls without an account and voters never need to sign up.

faq.q: Is Doodle's free plan still useful?
faq.a: Barely. The 1-active-poll limit and aggressive ads pushed most regular users to Pro at $6.95/mo.

faq.q: Which has better anti-fraud?
faq.a: Strawpoll, especially with login-required polls and CAPTCHA enabled. Doodle assumes participants are invited and trusted.

faq.q: Can I use both together?
faq.a: Yes — use Strawpoll to pick the activity, then Doodle to schedule when to do it.

a.name: Mentimeter
a.tagline: Interactive presentations with live audience polls

b.name: Slido
b.tagline: Q&A and live polls embedded in meetings and webinars

title: Mentimeter vs Slido (2026): Live Polls Showdown
description: Both run live audience polls during talks and meetings. Mentimeter wins on visual variety; Slido wins on Q&A and Microsoft integration. Full comparison.
intro: Mentimeter and Slido are the two heavyweights in live audience interaction. They overlap on polls, word clouds and quizzes, but diverge sharply on philosophy: Mentimeter wants to BE your slide deck; Slido wants to live inside it. We compared free limits, Q&A handling, integrations with PowerPoint, Teams and Webex, and how each scales to large audiences.

highlights.label: Q&A moderation
highlights.a: Basic Q&A; upvote support on paid plans
highlights.b: Industry-leading Q&A with moderation, labels, and replies

highlights.label: Poll types
highlights.a: 13+ slide types: word clouds, scales, ranking, 100-points, open text, quiz
highlights.b: 8 poll types including word cloud, rating, ranking, quiz

highlights.label: Presentation builder
highlights.a: Full deck-builder; can replace PowerPoint entirely
highlights.b: Lives inside PowerPoint/Google Slides/Keynote via add-in

highlights.label: Microsoft integration
highlights.a: Teams/PowerPoint add-ins available
highlights.b: Acquired by Cisco; deepest integration with Webex + native Teams/Outlook

highlights.label: Free tier
highlights.a: 2 questions per quiz, 5 quiz slides, unlimited participants
highlights.b: Free for up to 100 participants, 3 polls per event

highlights.label: Paid pricing
highlights.a: Basic $11.99/mo, Pro $24.99/mo (billed yearly)
highlights.b: Engage $12.50/mo, Professional $25/mo (billed yearly)

highlights.label: Audience size
highlights.a: Up to 10,000 on Enterprise
highlights.b: Up to 5,000 on Engage; higher on Enterprise

highlights.label: Visual polish
highlights.a: More themes, gradient backgrounds, brand customization
highlights.b: Cleaner, more corporate aesthetic

verdict: Mentimeter is the choice when the poll IS the presentation: keynotes, workshops, classrooms. Slido is the choice when polling supplements a meeting: town halls, webinars, Teams calls. Microsoft-stack companies almost always land on Slido. If neither fits, moomz is the lightweight free option for one-off social polls.
pickA: Pick Mentimeter if you want a full deck of interactive slides with rich visualizations, or you teach/train regularly.
pickB: Pick Slido if your audience is on Teams or Webex, you need serious Q&A moderation, or you already present in PowerPoint.

faq.q: Can Slido work without an internet connection?
faq.a: No. Both tools are fully cloud-based and require all participants to be online to vote.

faq.q: Which is better for university lectures?
faq.a: Mentimeter, generally. Its richer slide types and unlimited participants on free make it the academic default.

faq.q: Does Slido work outside Microsoft tools?
faq.a: Yes. There's a standalone web app and integrations with Google Slides, Keynote, Zoom and YouTube live streams.

faq.q: Can I export results?
faq.a: Both export to PDF and Excel on paid plans. Mentimeter also exports to PNG slides; Slido exports cleaner Q&A transcripts.

faq.q: Which has the bigger free tier?
faq.a: Mentimeter has more functional restrictions but unlimited participants; Slido has a cleaner free plan capped at 100 participants per event.

faq.q: Are votes anonymous on both?
faq.a: Yes by default on both. Slido can require name/email entry for moderated events.

a.name: Kahoot
a.tagline: Game-show style quizzes with music, points, and leaderboards

b.name: Mentimeter
b.tagline: Interactive presentations with live audience polls

title: Kahoot vs Mentimeter (2026): Quizzes or Polls?
description: Kahoot is built for game-show energy; Mentimeter is built for adult workshops and analytics. Both have a place — here's how to pick.
intro: Kahoot and Mentimeter both started as classroom tools, but they grew in opposite directions. Kahoot doubled down on game-show vibes — timers, music, leaderboards, the iconic 'k!' jingle. Mentimeter pivoted toward business and academia, prioritizing data export, anonymity, and varied poll types. We compared the two on quiz mechanics, audience size, pricing and corporate-readiness.

highlights.label: Game feel
highlights.a: Music, countdown timer, leaderboard, podium reveal at the end
highlights.b: More subdued; quiz mode exists but no soundtrack or podium

highlights.label: Poll variety
highlights.a: Mostly quiz: MCQ, true/false, puzzle, type-answer
highlights.b: 13+ types: word cloud, scales, 100-points, ranking, open text

highlights.label: K-12 adoption
highlights.a: Used in 90%+ of US K-12 schools per Kahoot's own data
highlights.b: Stronger in higher-ed and corporate training

highlights.label: Anonymous responses
highlights.a: Players pick a nickname; not truly anonymous (leaderboard names visible)
highlights.b: Fully anonymous by default; ideal for sensitive workshop topics

highlights.label: Data export
highlights.a: Reports as XLSX; limited filtering
highlights.b: PDF, PNG, XLSX; richer per-participant breakdowns

highlights.label: Free tier
highlights.a: Up to 40 players, unlimited quizzes, 4 question types
highlights.b: Unlimited participants, 2 questions per quiz, 5 slides per presentation

highlights.label: Pricing
highlights.a: Pro from $3.99/mo, Premier $9.99/mo (personal, billed yearly)
highlights.b: Basic $11.99/mo, Pro $24.99/mo (billed yearly)

highlights.label: Mobile native apps
highlights.a: Polished iOS/Android apps for both hosts and players
highlights.b: Web-only for hosting; players use a browser code

verdict: Kahoot owns the room when energy and competition matter — kids, team-building, icebreakers. Mentimeter owns the room when nuance, anonymity and analysis matter — workshops, training, executive offsites. Use the wrong one and you'll feel it within 60 seconds. If neither fits, moomz is the lightweight free option for one-off social polls.
pickA: Pick Kahoot for classrooms, team trivia, parties, onboarding games — anywhere a leaderboard adds fun rather than pressure.
pickB: Pick Mentimeter for workshops, town halls, surveys with multiple poll types, or sensitive topics where anonymity matters.

faq.q: Can Kahoot do non-quiz polls?
faq.a: Yes, there's a 'Poll' question type with no correct answer, but the platform is built around competitive quizzes.

faq.q: Is Mentimeter free for teachers?
faq.a: It has a free tier with unlimited participants but only 2 questions per quiz. Education plans start at $11.99/mo for full access.

faq.q: Which one has bigger audience caps?
faq.a: Mentimeter goes up to 10,000 on Enterprise. Kahoot scales similarly on its business plans (Kahoot+ Premier+ up to 2,000).

faq.q: Can students play Kahoot remotely?
faq.a: Yes via 'Challenge' mode where each player completes the quiz on their own schedule within a deadline.

faq.q: Does Mentimeter have a leaderboard?
faq.a: Yes, in Quiz mode. But the rest of the platform deliberately avoids competitive scoring to keep workshops collaborative.

faq.q: Which integrates with Zoom or Teams?
faq.a: Both. Mentimeter's Teams app is more mature; Kahoot's Zoom integration is widely used in K-12.

b.name: Typeform
b.tagline: Conversational forms with one question per screen

title: SurveyMonkey vs Typeform (2026): Which Survey Tool Wins?
description: SurveyMonkey is built for analysts and research teams; Typeform is built for marketers and lead gen. Both cost real money — here's the honest call.
intro: SurveyMonkey and Typeform are the two best-known paid survey tools, and they couldn't feel more different. SurveyMonkey looks like a corporate research tool from 1999 — because it is one. Typeform looks like a startup landing page from 2018. Underneath, they differ on statistical analysis, panel access, conditional logic and price-per-response. Here's how to choose.

highlights.label: Statistical analysis
highlights.a: Crosstabs, significance testing, weighting, sentiment analysis
highlights.b: Basic results dashboard; no built-in stats

highlights.label: Form design
highlights.a: Functional but dated UI
highlights.b: One-question-per-screen, custom themes, beautiful by default

highlights.label: Completion rates
highlights.a: Standard rates on long surveys (often 20-30%)
highlights.b: Reports ~57% average completion due to conversational flow

highlights.label: Panel access
highlights.a: SurveyMonkey Audience: buy targeted respondents (~$1-3 each)
highlights.b: No native panel; bring your own audience

highlights.label: Free tier
highlights.a: 10 questions, 40 responses per survey on free
highlights.b: 10 responses per month total on free

highlights.label: Paid pricing
highlights.a: Team Advantage from $25/user/mo (billed yearly)
highlights.b: Basic from $25/mo, Plus $50/mo (billed yearly)

highlights.label: Question library
highlights.a: 1,000+ pre-validated question templates
highlights.b: Smaller template library, more design-focused

highlights.label: Logic & branching
highlights.a: Skip logic, piping, randomization on paid plans
highlights.b: Branching, calculations, hidden fields, logic jumps on paid

verdict: If you're a research team running 50-question studies with quotas and crosstabs, SurveyMonkey is the obvious pick. If you're a marketing team running 5-question lead-gen forms where bounce rate is everything, Typeform earns its price tag. They barely compete head-to-head. If neither fits, moomz is the lightweight free option for one-off social polls.
pickA: Pick SurveyMonkey for academic research, market research, employee engagement studies, or anything needing statistical rigor.
pickB: Pick Typeform for lead capture, customer onboarding, product feedback, or any form where design quality affects conversion.

faq.q: Is SurveyMonkey really used by enterprises?
faq.a: Yes. 95% of Fortune 500 companies have a SurveyMonkey account per the company's own marketing. It dominates HR engagement surveys especially.

faq.q: Can I get statistical significance testing in Typeform?
faq.a: No, not natively. You'd export the data and run analysis in Excel, R, or a dedicated tool like Q Research Software.

faq.q: Which has better mobile experience?
faq.a: Typeform, decisively. One-question-per-screen was built for mobile from day one. SurveyMonkey is responsive but feels cramped.

faq.q: How much does SurveyMonkey Audience cost?
faq.a: Pricing is per-completed-response and varies by targeting tightness. Expect roughly $1 for general US adults, $3+ for niche B2B targeting.

faq.q: Can Typeform handle 100-question surveys?
faq.a: Technically yes, but engagement collapses past ~15 questions. SurveyMonkey is the better fit for long-form research.

faq.q: Do both offer GDPR-compliant hosting?
faq.a: Yes. SurveyMonkey has EU data residency on enterprise plans; Typeform offers EU hosting on Business and above.

a.name: JotForm
a.tagline: Drag-and-drop forms with 10,000+ templates and payment widgets

b.name: Google Forms
b.tagline: Free, unlimited surveys baked into Google Workspace

title: JotForm vs Google Forms (2026): Form Builder Comparison
description: JotForm wins on templates, design and payments. Google Forms wins on price and Sheets integration. Here's the breakdown for your use case.
intro: JotForm and Google Forms both target the same audience — small businesses and non-technical users who need to collect data — but they take very different approaches. JotForm sells design polish, payment widgets and a huge template library. Google Forms sells free, forever, with native Google integration. We compared free limits, payment processing, design, and which one actually replaces the other.

highlights.label: Free tier
highlights.a: 5 forms, 100 submissions/month, 100MB storage on free
highlights.b: Unlimited forms, unlimited responses, 15GB Drive included

highlights.label: Templates
highlights.a: 10,000+ pre-built templates across every industry
highlights.b: ~20 templates, mostly generic

highlights.label: Payment collection
highlights.a: 30+ payment gateways: Stripe, PayPal, Square, etc.
highlights.b: No native payments; requires add-on or workaround

highlights.label: Design flexibility
highlights.a: Drag-and-drop builder with full CSS access on paid
highlights.b: Material design only, limited theme options

highlights.label: Pricing
highlights.a: Bronze $34/mo, Silver $39/mo, Gold $99/mo (billed yearly)
highlights.b: Free forever; Workspace from $6/user/mo for admin features

highlights.label: Sheets integration
highlights.a: CSV export, Google Sheets via integration
highlights.b: Native live sync, pivot tables, instant collaboration

highlights.label: Conditional logic
highlights.a: Robust conditional logic, calculations, dynamic field updates
highlights.b: Basic section branching only

highlights.label: HIPAA compliance
highlights.a: Available on Gold ($99/mo) and Enterprise plans
highlights.b: Available on Workspace Enterprise with BAA

verdict: JotForm earns its price when you need payments, design polish, or industry-specific templates (medical intake, real estate, event registration). Google Forms wins when you just need answers and your team already lives in Workspace. The 100-submission cap on JotForm's free tier is where most people give up and move to Google Forms. If neither fits, moomz is the lightweight free option for one-off social polls.
pickA: Pick JotForm if you need payment processing, HIPAA compliance, conditional logic, or a polished template library to skip building from scratch.
pickB: Pick Google Forms if you want unlimited free submissions, native Sheets integration, and don't need payments or fancy design.

faq.q: Can Google Forms accept payments?
faq.a: Not directly. You can link out to Stripe Payment Links or PayPal, but there's no native payment widget like JotForm offers.

faq.q: Is JotForm's free plan usable for a small business?
faq.a: Maybe. 100 submissions/month is fine for low-volume contact forms but you'll hit the cap fast on event RSVPs or e-commerce.

faq.q: Which is better for HIPAA-compliant forms?
faq.a: Both can do it. JotForm Gold ($99/mo) is the cheaper entry point; Google requires a Workspace Enterprise plan with a signed BAA.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Google Forms to JotForm?
faq.a: Yes. JotForm has a Google Forms importer that converts most question types automatically.

faq.q: Which has better form analytics?
faq.a: JotForm provides built-in form analytics (drop-off rate, completion time). Google Forms shows basic response summaries; deeper analysis requires exporting to Sheets.

faq.q: Do both support file uploads?
faq.a: Yes. JotForm allows up to 1GB per file on paid plans; Google Forms requires respondents to sign in with a Google account to upload files.

a.name: Poll Everywhere
a.tagline: Live audience polls embedded in PowerPoint and Keynote

b.name: Slido
b.tagline: Q&A and live polls embedded in meetings and webinars

title: Poll Everywhere vs Slido (2026): Live Audience Tools
description: Poll Everywhere is the US-classroom default; Slido is the corporate-Teams default. Here's a feature-by-feature breakdown to pick the right one.
intro: Poll Everywhere and Slido are both serious players in live audience engagement, but they've grown around different ecosystems. Poll Everywhere has deep roots in US higher education and PowerPoint. Slido was acquired by Cisco and now lives inside Webex and Microsoft Teams. We compared free tiers, Q&A features, integrations, audience caps and pricing.

highlights.label: Q&A moderation
highlights.a: Basic Q&A on paid plans
highlights.b: Industry-leading Q&A with moderation, upvoting, labels, replies

highlights.label: PowerPoint integration
highlights.a: Native PowerPoint add-in widely used in US universities
highlights.b: Add-in available but Slido leans on Teams/Webex first

highlights.label: Poll types
highlights.a: Multiple choice, word cloud, Q&A, clickable image, ranking, open-ended
highlights.b: Multiple choice, word cloud, rating, ranking, quiz, open text

highlights.label: Microsoft/Cisco integration
highlights.a: Generic Teams/Zoom integrations
highlights.b: Native to Webex (owned by Cisco), deepest Teams integration

highlights.label: Free tier
highlights.a: Up to 25 responses per activity on free
highlights.b: Up to 100 participants and 3 polls per event on free

highlights.label: Higher-ed pricing
highlights.a: Faculty plans from $14/mo (250 responses), LMS integration included
highlights.b: Educational pricing exists but more enterprise-focused

highlights.label: Standard paid pricing
highlights.a: Present from $14/mo, Engage $46/mo (billed yearly)
highlights.b: Engage $12.50/mo, Professional $25/mo (billed yearly)

highlights.label: LMS integration
highlights.a: Canvas, Blackboard, Brightspace, Moodle grade passback
highlights.b: No native LMS integration; aimed at enterprise meetings

verdict: Poll Everywhere is the no-brainer for US universities and any institution running PowerPoint lectures with LMS grade passback. Slido is the no-brainer for companies on Webex or Microsoft Teams who care about Q&A moderation more than polls. They rarely directly compete because their core audiences barely overlap. If neither fits, moomz is the lightweight free option for one-off social polls.
pickA: Pick Poll Everywhere if you're in higher education, your students use clickers, or you need LMS grade integration with Canvas or Blackboard.
pickB: Pick Slido if your team lives in Teams or Webex, you run town halls, or Q&A moderation is more important than poll variety.

faq.q: Does Poll Everywhere work with clickers?
faq.a: Yes. It supports physical TurningPoint and Reef clickers, plus 'BYOD' phone responses. Slido is web-only.

faq.q: Which is better for university classes?
faq.a: Poll Everywhere, almost always. LMS integration with Canvas/Blackboard for grade passback is the killer feature for instructors.

faq.q: Can Slido replace Poll Everywhere for K-12?
faq.a: Not really. Slido lacks the LMS integrations and clicker support that schools rely on. Kahoot or Poll Everywhere are better K-12 fits.

faq.q: Which has the best Q&A feature?
faq.a: Slido, decisively. Its Q&A moderation, upvoting and labeling system is what made it a Cisco acquisition target.

faq.q: How do they handle large audiences?
faq.a: Both scale to thousands on Enterprise. Poll Everywhere caps at 700 on standard plans; Slido caps at 5,000 on Engage.

faq.q: Is Poll Everywhere free for students?
faq.a: Yes — students respond for free. The cost is on the instructor or institution depending on the plan.
